Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - LEARNING WHEN AND WHAT TO ASK: A HIERARCHI CAL REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FRAMEWORK

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Reliable AI agents should be mindful of the limits of their knowledge and consult humans when sensing that they do not have sufficient knowledge to make sound decisions. We formulate a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for learning to decide _when_ to request additional information from humans and _what_ type of information would be helpful to request. Our framework extends partially-observed Markov decision processes (POMDPs) by allowing an agent to interact with an assistant to leverage their knowledge in accomplishing tasks. Results on a simulated human-assisted navigation problem demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework: aided with an interaction policy learned by our method, a navigation policy achieves up to a 7 _×_ improvement in task success rate compared to performing tasks only by itself. We find that the ability to request subgoals enables the agent to generalize effectively to tasks in unseen environments. We analyze benefits and challenges of learning with a hierarchical policy structure and suggest directions for future work.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Human-agent communication at deployment time has been under-explored in machine learning, where the traditional focus has been on building agents that can accomplish tasks on their own (full autonomy). Nevertheless, enabling an agent to exchange information with humans during its operation can potentially enhance its helpfulness and trustworthiness. The ability to request and interpret human advice would help the agent accomplish tasks beyond its built-in knowledge, while the ability to accurately convey when and why it is about to fail would make the agent safer to use.

In his classical work, Grice (1975) outlines the desired characteristics of cooperative communication, commonly known as the _Gricean maxims of cooperation_ . Among these characteristics are informativeness (the maxim of quantity) and faithfulness (the maxim of quality). Human-agent communication in current work has fallen short in these two aspects. Traditional frameworks like imitation learning and reinforcement learning employ limited communication protocols where the agent and the human can only exchange simple intentions (requesting low-level actions or rewards Torrey & Taylor (2013); Knox & Stone (2009)). More powerful frameworks like (Nguyen & Daum´e III, 2019; Nguyen et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2019) allow the agent to process high-level instructions from humans, but the agent still only requests generic help. Recent work in natural language processing endows the agent with the ability to generate rich natural language utterances (Camburu et al., 2018; Rao & Daum´e III, 2018; De Vries et al., 2017; Das et al., 2017), but the communication is not faithful in the sense that the agent only mirrors human-generated utterances without grounding its communication in self-perception of its (in)capabilities and (un)certainties. Essentially it learns to convey what a human may be concerned about, not what _it_ is concerned about.

This paper presents a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework named HARI ( **H** uman- **A** ssisted **R** einforced **I** nteraction), which supports richer and more faithful human-agent communication. Our framework allows the agent to learn to convey intrinstic needs for specific information and to incorporate diverse types of information from humans to make better decisions. Specifically, the agent in HARI is equipped with three information-seeking intentions: in every step, it can choose to request more information about (i) its current state, (ii) the goal state, or (iii) a subgoal state which, if reached, helps it make progress on the current task. Upon receiving a request, the human can transfer new information to the agent by giving new descriptions of the requested state. These de-

An assistant communicates with the agent and can provide it with information about the environment and the task. Initially (A) it may request more information about the goal, but may not know enough about where it currently is. For example, at location B, due to limited perception, it does not recognize that it is in a living room and stands next to a couch. It can obtain such information from the assistant. If the current task becomes too difficult (like at location C), the agent can require the assistant to provide a simpler subtask which, if accomplished, helps it make progress on the main task. The agent maintains a stack of tasks and always executes the task at the top. When the agent receives a (sub)task, it pushes the (sub)task to the top of the stack. When it wants to stop executing a (sub)task, it pops the (sub)task from the stack (e.g., at location D). At location E, the agent empties the stack and terminates its execution.

scriptions will be incorporated as new inputs to the agent’s decision-making policy. The human thus can transfer any form of information that can be interpreted by the policy (e.g., asking the agent to execute skills that it has learned, giving side information that connects the agent to a situation it is more familiar with). Because the agent’s policy can implement a variety of model architectures and learning algorithms, our framework opens up many possibilities for human-agent communication.

To enable faithful communication, we teach the agent to understand its intrinsic needs by interacting with the human and the environment (rather than imitating human behaviors). By requesting different types of information and observing how much each type of information enhances its decisions, the agent gradually learns to determine which information is most useful to obtain in a given situation. With this capability, at deployment time, it can choose when and what information to ask from the human to improve its task performance. To demonstrate the effectiveness of HARI, we simulate a human-assisted navigation problem where an agent has access to only sparse information about its current state and the goal, and can request additional information about these states. On tasks that take place in previously unseen environments, the ability to ask for help improves the agent’s success rate by 7 _×_ higher compared to performing tasks only by itself. This human-assisted agent even outperforms an agent that always has access to dense information in unseen environments, thanks to the ability to request subgoals. We show that performance of the agent can be further improved by recursively asking for subgoals of subgoals. We discuss limitations of the policy’s model and feature representation, which suggest room for future improvements.

## 3 LEVERAGING HUMAN KNOWLEDGE TO BETTER ACCOMPLISH TASKS

We introduce an _assistant_ into the operation environment, who can provide information about the environment’s states. We assume the agent possesses a pre-learned operation policy _π_ ˆ. This policy serves as the _common ground_ between the agent and the assistant, which is a prerequisite for communication between them to occur. For example, this policy represents a set of basic tasks that agent has mastered and the assistant can ask the agent to perform. In general, the more knowledge encoded in this policy, the more effectively the agent can communicate with and leverage help from the assistant. Our goal is to learn an _interaction policy ψθ_ (parametrized by _θ_ ) that controls how the agent communicates with the assistant to gather additional information. The operation policy _π_ ˆ will be invoked by the interaction policy if the latter decides that the agent does not need new information and wants to take an operating action.

The assistant aids the agent by giving new (current or goal) state descriptions, connecting the agent to situations on which it can make better decisions. Consider an object-finding navigation problem, where a robot has been trained to reliably navigate to the kitchen from the living room of a house. Suppose the robot is then asked to “ _find a mug_ ”, an object that it has never heard of. The assistant can help the robot accomplish this task by giving a more informative goal description “ _find a mug in the kitchen_ ”, relating the current task to the kitchen-finding task that the robot has been familiar with. The robot may also have problems with localization: it knows how to get the kitchen from the living room but it may not realize that it is currently the living room. In this case, giving a currentstate description that specifies this information provides the robot with a useful hint on what actions to take next.
Our framework allows the assistant to convey any form of information that the agent can incorporate into its input. As discussed in §2, the notion of “state description” in our framework is general,

Communication between the agent and the assistant can be flexibly enriched by designing the agent’s operation policy to be able to consume the forms of information of interest (e.g., a policy that takes natural language as input).

**Communication with the Assistant.** The assistant is present all the time and knows the agent’s current state _st_ and the goal state _gt_ . It is specified by two functions: a description function _ρA_ : _S × D →_ ∆( _D_ ) and a subgoal function _ωA_ : _S × S →_ ∆( _S_ ). _ρA_ ( _d[′] | s, d_ ) specifies the probability of giving _d[′]_ as the new description of state _s_ given a current description _d_ . _ωA_ ( _g[′] | s, g_ ) indicates the probability of proposing _g[′]_ as a subgoal given a current state _s_ and a goal state _g_ .

At time step _t_ , the assistant accepts three types of request from the agent:

- (a) CUR: requests a new description of _st_ and receives _d[s] t_ +1 _[∼][ρ][A]_[(] _[· |][ s][t][, d][s] t_[)][;] (b) GOAL: requests a new description of _gt_ and receives _d[g] t_ +1 _[∼][ρ][A]_[ (] _[· |][ g][t][, d] t[g]_[)][;] (c) SUB: requests a description of a subgoal _gt_ +1 and receives _d[g] t_ +1 _[∼][ρ][A]_[ (] _[· |][ g][t]_[+1] _[,][ ∅]_[)][ where] _gt_ +1 _∼ ωA_ ( _· | st, gt_ ) and _∅_ is an empty description.

- **Interaction Policy.** The action space of the interaction policy _ψθ_ consists of five actions: _{_ CUR _,_ GOAL _,_ SUB _,_ DO _,_ DONE _}_ . The first three actions correspond to making the three types of request that the assistants accepts. The remaining two actions are used to traverse in the operation environment: (d) DO: executes the action _a_[do] _t_ ≜ arg max _a∈A_ ˆ _π_ ( _a | b[s] t[, d] t[g]_[)][.][The agent transitions to a new] operation state _st_ +1 _∼ T_ ( _st, a_[do] _t_[)][;]

- (e) DONE: determines that the current goal _gt_ has been reached.[2] If _gt_ is a main goal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the task episode ends. If _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the agent may choose a new goal to follow. Our problem formulation leaves it open on what goal should be selected next.

By selecting among these actions, the interaction policy essentially decides _when_ to ask the assistant for additional information, and _what_ types of information to ask for. Our formulation does not specify the input space of the interaction policy, as this space depends on how the agent implements its goal memory (i.e. how it stores and retrieves the subgoals). In the next section, we introduce an instantiation where the agent uses a stack data structure to manage (sub)goals.

## 4 HIERARCHICAL REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FRAMEWORK

In this section, we describe the HARI framework. We first formulate the POMDP environment that the interaction policy acts in, referred to as the _interaction environment_ (§4.1). Our construction employs a _goal stack_ to manage multiple levels of (sub)goals (§4.2). A goal stack stores all the tasks the agent has been assigned but has not yet decided to terminate (by choosing the DONE action). It is updated in every step depending on the taken action. We design a cost function (§4.4) that specifies a trade-off between the cost of taking actions (acting cost) and the cost of not completing a task (task error).

## 4.4 COST FUNCTION

The interaction policy needs to balance between two types of cost: the cost of taking actions (acting cost) and the cost of not completing a task (task error). The acting cost also subsumes the cost of communicating with the assistant because, in reality, such interactions consume time, human effort, and possibly trust. Assuming that the assistant is helpful, acting cost and task error usually conflict with each other; for example, the agent may lower its task error if it is willing to suffer a larger acting cost by increasing the number of requests to the assistant.

We employ a simplified model where all types of cost are non-negative real numbers of the same unit. Making a request of type _a_ is assigned a constant cost _γa_ . The cost of taking the DO action is _c_ ( _st, a_[do] _t_[)][,][the][cost][of][executing][the] _[a]_[do] _t_[action][in][the][operation][environment.][Calling][D][ONE][to] terminate execution of the main goal _g_ 1 incurs a task error _c_ ( _st, a_ done). We exclude the task errors

## 5 LEARNING WHEN AND WHAT TO ASK IN HUMAN-ASSISTED NAVIGATION

**Problem.** We apply HARI to modeling a human-assisted navigation (HAN) problem. In HAN, a human requests an agent to find an object in an indoor environment. Each task request asks the agent to go to a room of type _r_ and find an object of type _o_ (e.g., find a mug in a kitchen). The agent is equipped with a camera and shares its camera view with the human. We assume that the human is sufficiently familiar with the environment that they can recognize the agent’s location by looking at its current view. Before issuing a task request, the human imagines a goal location (but do not reveal it to the agent). We are primarily interested in evaluating success in _goal-finding_ , i.e. whether the agent can arrive at the human’s intended goal location. Even though there could be multiple locations that match a request, the agent only succeeds if it arrives exactly at the chosen goal location. We also determine success in _request-fulfilling_ , where the agent successfully fulfills a request if it navigates to any node that is within two meters of an object that matches the request.

While an agent is performing a task, it may request the human to provide additional information via telecommunication (e.g., a chat app). Specifically, it can ask for a description of its current location (CUR), the goal location (GOAL), or a subgoal location that is on the path from its current location to the goal location (SUB). Detail about how the subgoals are determined is in the Appendix.

**Operation Environment.** We construct the operation environments using the environment graphs provided by the Matterport3D simulator (Anderson et al., 2018). Each environment graph is generated from a 3D model of a house where each node is a location in the house and each edge connects two nearby unobstructed locations. Each operation state _s_ corresponds to a node in the graph. At any time, the agent’s operation action space _A_ consists of traversing to any of the nodes that are adjacent to its current node.

We employ a discrete bag-of-features representation for state descriptions.[3] A bag of features represents the information that the agent extracts from the raw input that the agent perceives (e.g., an image, a language sentence). Working with this intermediate input allows us to easily vary the type and amount of information given to the agent. Specifically, we simulate two settings of descriptions: _dense_ and _sparse_ . At evaluation time, the agent perceives sparse descriptions and request the assistant for dense descriptions. A dense description of a current location contains the room name at the location, and the features of _M_ objects restricted to be within _δ_ meters of the location. The features of each object consists of (i) its name, (ii) horizontal and vertical angles (relative to the current viewpoint), and (iii) distance (in meters) from the object to the current location. A dense description of a goal follows the same representation scheme. In the sparse setting, the current-location description does not include the room name. Moreover, we remove the features of objects that are not in the top 100 most frequent objects, emulating an imperfect object detector module. The sparse goal description (the task request) has only features of the target object and the room name where the object is located at. Especially, if a subgoal location is adjacent or coincides with the agent’s current location, instead of describing room and object features, the human specifies the ground-truth action to go to the subgoal (an action is specified by its horizontal and vertical angles, and travel distance).[4]

**Experimental Procedure.** We conduct our experiments in three phases. In the _pre-training_ phase, we learn an operation policy _π_ ˆ with dense descriptions of the current location and the goal. In the _training_ phase, the agent perceives a sparse description of its current location and is given a sparse initial goal description. We use advantage actor-critic (Mnih et al., 2016) to learn an interaction policy _ψθ_ that controls how the agent communicates with the human and navigates in an environment.

3While our representation of state descriptions simplifies the object/room detection problem for the agent, it does not necessarily make the navigation problem easier than with image input, as images may contain information that is not captured by our representation (e.g., object shapes and colors, visualization of paths). 4Here, we emulate a practical scenario where if a destination is visible in the current view, to save effort, a human would concisely tell an agent what to do rather than giving a verbose description of the destination.

The human always returns dense descriptions. The interaction policy is trained in environments that are previously seen as well as unseen during pre-training. Finally, in the _evaluation_ phase, the interaction policy is tested on three conditions: seen environment and target object type but starting from a new room (UNSEENSTR), seen environment but new target object type (UNSEENOBJ), and new environment (UNSEENENV). The pre-trained operation policy _π_ ˆ is fixed during the training and evaluation phases. We create 82,104 examples for pre-training, 65,133 for training, and approximately 2,000 for each validation or test set. Details about the training procedure and the dataset are included in the Appendix.

## 6 RESULTS AND ANALYSES

**Settings.** In our main experiments, we set: the cost of taking a CUR, GOAL, SUB, or DO action to be 0.01 (we will consider other settings subsequently), the cost of calling DONE to terminate the main goal (i.e. task error) equal the (unweighted) length of the shortest-path from the agent’s location to the goal, and the goal stack’s size ( _L_ ) to be 2. We compare our RL-learned interaction policy with a rule-based baseline that first takes the GOAL action and then randomly selects actions. In each episode, we enforce that the rule-based policy can take at most _⌊Xa⌋_ + _y_ actions of type _a_ , where _y ∼_ Bernoulli( _Xa −⌊Xa⌋_ ) and _Xa_ is a constant. We tune each _Xa_ on the validation sets so that the rule-based policy has the same average count of each action as the RL-learned policy. To prevent early termination, we enforce that the rule-based policy cannot take more DONE actions than SUB actions unless when its SUB action’s budget is exhausted. We also construct a skyline where the interaction policy is also learned by RL but with an operation policy that executes subgoals perfectly. As discussed in §5, we are primarily interested in goal-finding success rate and will refer to this metric briefly as success rate.

**Main Results (Table 1).** To inspect the potential benefits of asking for additional information, we compute how much the operation policy _π_ ˆ improves when it is supplied with dense information about the current and/or goal states. As seen, success rate of the operation policy is lifted dramatically when both the current-state and goal descriptions are dense ( _∼_ 2 _×_ increase on UNSEENSTR, _∼_ 5 _×_ on UNSEENOBJ, and _∼_ 3 _×_ on UNSEENENV). We find that dense information about the current state is more helpful on UNSEENSTR, while dense information about the goal is more valuable on UNSEENOBJ and UNSEENENV. This is reasonable because on UNSEENSTR, the agent has been trained to find similar goals. In contrast, the initial goal descriptions in UNSEENOBJ and UNSEENENV are completely new to the agent, thus gathering more information about them is necessary.

Aided by our RL-learned interaction policy, the agent observes a substantial _∼_ 2 _×_ increase in success rate on UNSEENSTR, _∼_ 5 _×_ on UNSEENOBJ, and _∼_ 7 _×_ on UNSEENENV, compared to when performing tasks using only the operation policy. In unseen environments, with its capability of requesting subgoals, the agent impressively doubles the success rate of the operation policy that has access to dense descriptions. ## 7 RELATED WORK AND CONCLUSION

**Transfer Learning in Reinforcement Learning.** Various frameworks have been proposed to model knowledge transfer from a more capable agent to a novice one (Da Silva & Costa, 2019). Torrey & Taylor (2013) introduce the action-advising framework where a learner strategically requests reference actions from a teacher. Da Silva et al. (2020) investigate uncertainty-based strategies for deciding when to request in this framework. In an agent-to-agent setting, (Da Silva et al., 2017; Zimmer et al., 2014; Omidshafiei et al., 2019) focus on learning a teaching policy in addition to an advice-requesting policy. An important assumption in these papers is that the teacher must share a common action space with the learner. More recent frameworks (Kim et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2019; Nguyen & Daum´e III, 2019) relax this assumption by allowing the teacher to specify high-level subgoals instead of low-level actions. HARI can be viewed as a strict extension of these frameworks. It allows the human to specify not only subgoals, but also any additional information about the current state and the goal that agent can interpret. Moreover, HARI equips the agent with _multiple_ communication intentions and teaches it to select the most useful intention to convey in a given situation. Another line of work employs standard RL communication protocol, where the human can only transfer knowledge through numerical scores or categorical feedback (Knox & Stone, 2009; Judah et al., 2010; Peng et al., 2016; Griffith et al., 2013). Maclin & Shavlik (1996) propose a framework where the human advises the agent using a domain-specific language, specifying rules that can be incorporated into the agent’s model. In contrast, HARI operates with a black-box agent model. Sumers et al. (2020) extract features from various types of language feedback to construct a reward function for reinforcement learning. We instead focus on deployment-time communication and directly incorporate the human feedback as input to agent’s operation policy.

**Task-Oriented Dialog and Generating Natural Language Questions.** HARI models a taskoriented dialog problem. Many variants of this problem requires the agent to compose specific questions (De Vries et al., 2017; Das et al., 2017; Thomason et al., 2020). The dominant approach in these problems is to mimic pre-collected human utterances. As discussed previously, naively mirroring human external behavior cannot enable agents to understand the limits of their knowledge. We teach the agent to understand its intrinsic needs through interaction with the human and the environment rather than through imitation of human behaviors. Another related line of work concerns generating natural language explanations of model decisions (Camburu et al., 2018; Hendricks et al., 2016; Rajani et al., 2019).

In summary, this paper presents a general POMDP framework for modeling human-agent communication. While we demonstrate this framework on a simplified navigation problem, our framework can theoretically capture richer types of human-agent communication. Hence, an important empirical question is how well our formulation generalizes to richer environments with more complex interactions and state spaces (Shridhar et al., 2020). Enhancing the sample efficiency of the learning policy by exploiting the hierarchical policy structure is an exciting future direction. Furthermore, techniques for generating faithful explanations (Kumar & Talukdar, 2020; Madsen et al., 2021) can be applied to enhance the specificity of the generated questions.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and conceptually innovative hierarchical reinforcement learning framework—HARI—for enabling agents to learn both *when* and *what* to ask from humans during task execution. The work makes a strong contribution by extending the standard POMDP formulation to support structured, goal-driven communication, addressing a critical gap in human-agent collaboration: the need for faithful, self-aware, and contextually appropriate information-seeking behavior. The writing is generally clear and logically organized, with a thoughtful progression from motivation through technical formulation to empirical evaluation. The use of Gricean maxims as a guiding principle for designing cooperative communication lends philosophical rigor and underscores the paper’s emphasis on *intrinsic*, needs-based queries rather than superficial imitation of human language patterns. The experimental results are compelling, particularly the 7× improvement in task success rate and the demonstration of generalization to unseen environments via subgoal requests. However, a major limitation lies in the lack of clarity around the model’s architecture and learning algorithm—specifically, how the hierarchical policy is trained end-to-end (or whether it relies on manual decomposition), and how uncertainty or "self-perception" is formally operationalized within the agent. While the framework claims to enable faithful communication grounded in the agent’s own limitations, the mechanisms by which the agent learns to recognize these limitations (e.g., via internal belief states, prediction errors, or auxiliary losses) are under-specified. Additionally, the simulation environment appears highly abstracted, and the nature of human responses (i.e., how descriptions are generated and parsed) is not sufficiently detailed, raising concerns about the realism and reproducibility of the setup. These omissions weaken the technical grounding of an otherwise promising and impactful approach. Strengthening the methodological exposition and providing clearer distinctions between the policy components (e.g., meta-controller for information requests vs. worker policy for task execution) would significantly improve the paper’s scientific rigor and accessibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about success rate improvement in abstract and introduction
comments.quote: Results on a simulated human-assisted navigation problem demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework: aided with an interaction policy learned by our method, a navigation policy achieves up to a 7 _×_ improvement in task success rate compared to performing tasks only by itself.
comments.explanation: At first, the claim of a 7× improvement in task success rate seems clear and impactful. However, later in the introduction, the same result is restated as 'the ability to ask for help improves the agent’s success rate by 7 _×_ higher compared to performing tasks only by itself.' The use of 'by 7× higher' is problematic: 'improves by 7×' typically means an 8-fold absolute increase (i.e., original + 7× original), while 'improves to 7×' or 'is 7× higher' would mean a 7-fold increase. The phrase 'by 7× higher' is linguistically incoherent—it combines additive and multiplicative phrasings. Given that both the abstract and the introduction use similar but slightly different phrasings ('7× improvement' vs '7× higher'), it raises concern about whether the authors mean the success rate is multiplied by 7, or increased by 7 times its value (i.e., 8× the baseline). While '7× improvement' is often interpreted in practice as '7 times the baseline' (not '+7×'), the later phrasing 'by 7× higher' suggests potential confusion in quantification. This is not resolved by context because both statements are central empirical claims. A careful reader might misinterpret the magnitude of the gain, potentially inflating the perceived performance. Standard usage in ML literature (e.g., 'n× improvement') usually means multiplicative scaling, so the first statement is acceptable, but the second wording undermines precision.

comments.title: Ambiguity in subgoal request mechanism and its modeling
comments.quote: The agent in HARI is equipped with three information-seeking intentions: in every step, it can choose to request more information about (i) its current state, (ii) the goal state, or (iii) a subgoal state which, if reached, helps it make progress on the current task.
comments.explanation: The prose suggests that the agent can request information about a subgoal state—but a subgoal has not yet been defined at this point in the text. This initially creates confusion: is the agent requesting information about an existing subgoal, or asking the assistant to *propose* a subgoal? The latter is clearly the intended meaning, as later sections (e.g., §3 and §4.2) clarify that the SUB action triggers the assistant to propose a new subgoal via ωA. However, the phrasing 'request more information about... a subgoal state' incorrectly implies the subgoal already exists and the agent wants more details on it, rather than initiating the creation of a new subgoal. This misleads the reader into thinking the subgoal is pre-specified or under the agent’s control, when in fact it is sampled by the assistant. Context eventually resolves this, but in a key conceptual description like this—especially in the introduction—it’s important to be precise. A better phrasing would be 'request the assistant to propose a helpful subgoal (a state which, if reached, helps...)' to reflect agency lies with the assistant.

comments.title: Misleading figure caption description of stack behavior
comments.quote: When the agent receives a (sub)task, it pushes the (sub)task to the top of the stack. When it wants to stop executing a (sub)task, it pops the (sub)task from the stack (e.g., at location D).
comments.explanation: The caption says the agent pops a (sub)task 'when it wants to stop executing' it, implying that DONE pops any arbitrary subtask on demand. However, later formal definition in §4.2 states that DONE only pops the top task on the stack—consistent with stack semantics. The phrase 'wants to stop executing a (sub)task' is ambiguous: it could imply the agent can select which subtask to terminate, breaking stack discipline. But the example at location D shows popping the current (top) subtask, and the full mechanism uses a stack with LIFO order. Thus, the intended behavior is consistent, but the wording in the caption could mislead a reader into thinking the agent has random access to subtasks on the stack. Given that the formal model is correct and the figure example is accurate, the issue is one of phrasing. Still, for a reader relying on the caption before seeing the formalism, the potential for misunderstanding is real, especially given real-world agents sometimes allow pausing/resuming arbitrary subroutines.

comments.title: Use of 'gt = g1' in description of DONE action suggests logical error
comments.quote: If _gt_ is a main goal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the task episode ends. If _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the agent may choose a new goal to follow.
comments.explanation: This passage from section 3 creates a serious concern: it says 'if _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1)', but _g_ 1 is defined as the main goal. This would imply a subgoal equals the main goal, which is contradictory. Initially, this appears to be a typo—likely the second equality should be _gt_ ≠ _g_ 1. Checking the context, the first clause correctly identifies main goal with _gt_ = _g_ 1. The second clause cannot mean _gt_ = _g_ 1 if it is a subgoal, unless the subgoal is the main goal, which defeats the purpose. However, the parentheses suggest an attempt to define the condition, not describe a subcase. Given the stack mechanism in §4.2, subgoals are pushed onto the stack and become _gt_, while _g_ 1 remains the original goal. Therefore, subgoals are by definition different from _g_ 1 (or at least not required to be equal). This is likely a transcription or OCR error where '≠' was misread as '='. Even under most plausible correction (≠), the syntax 'gt is a subgoal (gt ≠ g1)' would still be awkward. But without correction, it’s logically inconsistent. Since the rest of the framework assumes _g_ 1 ≠ subgoals and stack-based execution depends on distinguishing them, this is a real notational error. However, due to clear context and figure, the intent is recoverable. Still, it’s a moderate risk for confusion.

comments.title: Misleading implication about fixed description function in standard POMDP
comments.quote: Moreover, the description function _ρ_ and the goal description _d[g]_ 1[are assumed to be fixed during a task execution.
comments.explanation: The assertion that _ρ_ is fixed is standard in traditional POMDPs, but in this paper, _ρ_ has been redefined as a general 'description function' that includes communication-mediated inputs (per footnote [1]). The authors correctly distinguish their use of 'description' from traditional 'observation', but here they conflate the interpretation: in a standard POMDP, _ρ_ is fixed *by definition*, but in HARI, _ρ_ is supplemented by _ρA_, creating a time-varying effective description process. The sentence risks leading a reader to believe that *in HARI* the original _ρ_ remains the only source of descriptions and is immutable, when in fact _ρ_ coexists with _ρA_ as complementary sources. However, since the paper clearly separates _ρ_ (sensor-based) and _ρA_ (assistant-based), and later defines agent requests as accessing _ρA_, the confusion is resolved by context. Still, the phrasing could imply that _ρ_ itself is modified, which it is not. A more precise statement would clarify that _ρ_ is fixed, but the agent can access additional descriptions via _ρA_ through requests.

comments.quote: Given _d[s] t_ and _d[g] t_[,][the][agent][then][makes][a][decision] _[a][t][∈A]_[,][transitions][to][the][next][state] _[s][t]_[+1] _[∼][T]_[(] _[s][t][, a][t]_[)][,] and receives a cost _ct_ ≜ _c_ ( _st, at_ )
comments.explanation: The agent receives a description _d[s]_t ∼ ρ(s_t), makes a decision, and transitions to s_{t+1}. However, the next description _d[s]_{t+1}_ is not yet observed at the time of action selection, so _d[s]_t should be the only state description input at time _t_. This is standard in POMDPs. The text correctly implies this causal order, but the use of _d[g]_t and _d[s]_t together as input is not explicitly reconciled with the fact that goal description updates only occur via GOAL or SUB requests. There is no error per se — _d[g]_t is maintained separately and updated on request — but the passage does not clarify how _d[g]_t evolves stochastically via _ρA_ only upon requests. Without this, a reader might assume _d[g]_t is always updated autonomously. Fortunately, the definition of the assistant's _ρA_ and request types later provides sufficient detail. comments.title: Underspecification of subgoal function domain
comments.quote: _ωA_ ( _g[′] | s, g_ ) indicates the probability of proposing _g[′]_ as a subgoal given a current state _s_ and a goal state _g_ .
comments.explanation: The subgoal function _ωA_ : S × S → ∆(S) is used to sample _g_{t+1}_ given _s_t_ and _g_t_. While this is well-defined, it is not clarified whether _g_{t+1}_ must lie on a path from _s_t_ to _g_t_, or if it can be arbitrary. In the navigation domain, subgoals are path-intermediate (per Appendix mention), so the function should reflect path coherence. But here, _ωA_ is presented as a fully general stochastic function, raising questions about the risk of proposing irrelevant or counterproductive subgoals (e.g., a subgoal behind the agent). The effectiveness claim in the abstract (generalization via subgoals) depends on meaningful subgoal selection, but no constraints or priors on _ωA_ are specified in this section. While later context (Appendix reference and Figure 1) implies structured subgoal generation, the formalism as presented here leaves this critical component underspecified.

The current wording creates a serious logical flaw in a core behavioral rule of the interaction policy.

comments.title: Ambiguous factorization of transition function _T¯_ without full specification
comments.quote: State-transition function _T_[¯] = _Ts · TG_ where _Ts_ : _S × D × A_[¯] _→_ ∆( _S × D_ ) and _TG_ : _GL × A →_[¯] ∆( _GL_ );
comments.explanation: The notation '_T¯ = Ts · TG_' suggests a product of distributions (perhaps independent factors), but no clarification is given about how the joint transition over (_S × D_) and _GL_ is composed. In standard POMDP or MDP formulations, when state spaces are factored, the transition function is typically either factored multiplicatively (under independence) or defined via a joint update. Here, the dot ('·') operator is undefined, and it's unclear whether _Ts_ and _TG_ operate in parallel or sequentially. While §4.3 begins to clarify _Ts_, it only gives qualitative descriptions like 'Only taking the DO action may change the current operation state' and does not provide a formal expression for _Ts_ or how it interacts with _TG_. Given that _TG_ is defined as a deterministic update via an 'update' function in §4.2, but _Ts_ governs probabilistic transitions in _S_, the composition remains underspecified. This makes it difficult to reproduce the environment dynamics, especially since the interaction policy's learning depends on this structure. The rest of the section assumes this transition is well-defined, but a reader cannot reconstruct _T¯_ from the given description.

comments.title: Description space _D¯_ incorrectly defined with fixed size _L_
comments.quote: - Description space _D_[¯] = _D × GL[d]_[where] _[ G] L[d]_[is the set of all goal-description stacks of size] _[ L]_
comments.explanation: The text defines _GL[d]_ as 'the set of all goal-description stacks of size _L_', but in §4.2, _GL_ is defined as stacks of _at most L elements_. This is inconsistent: if the stack can have variable length (from 0 to L), then the description stack _G[d]_ should also be variable-length. However, defining _D¯ = D × GL[d]_ with _GL[d]_ as fixed-size stacks would incorrectly require padding or truncation. For instance, when the stack is empty or has only one element, the description space would still require _L_ descriptions. This creates a mismatch between _S¯_ (which can hold stacks of variable length up to L) and _D¯_ (which assumes fixed size L). The description should depend on the actual stack length. While OCR might explain 'size L' vs 'up to L', the original definition in §4.2 clearly uses variable length ('at most _L_ elements'), so this is a logical inconsistency. The deterministic description function _ρ¯(s, d[s], G) = (d[s], G[d]_) only makes sense if _G[d]_ is the description sequence of the current stack, not a fixed-size object.

comments.title: Cost of DONE action conflates task error with acting cost
comments.quote: Calling DONE to terminate execution of the main goal _g_ 1 incurs a task error _c_ ( _st, a_ done). We exclude the task errors of executing subgoals because the interaction policy is only evaluated on reaching the main goal.
comments.explanation: The cost of taking DONE is said to be a task error _c(st, a_done), but _c_ is previously defined in the operation environment as a cost function _c: S × A → ℝ_ that applies to any action, including _a_done_. However, in the context of the interaction environment, the cost function ¯c is supposed to include both acting cost and task error. The passage implies that _c(st, a_done)_ is used directly as the task error, but this is ambiguous: is this cost only incurred upon incorrect termination, or always? The term 'task error' suggests it should depend on whether _gt_ has truly been reached, but the expression _c(st, a_done)_ does not reflect success or failure—it's just the intrinsic cost of the DONE action. A more coherent setup would define task error as a function of state proximity to the goal, not reuse the operation cost. The authors likely intend _c(st, a_done)_ as a base acting cost, while task error is an additional penalty for failure. But the current wording conflates the two, and the exclusion of task errors for subgoals lacks a formal mechanism—simply saying 'we exclude' is insufficient without showing how the cost function reflects that.

comments.title: Ambiguity in subgoal generation timing and stack update
comments.quote: The SUB action pushes a new subtask ( _gt_ +1 _, d[g] t_ +1[)][ to the] stack. ... _gt_ +1 ∼ _ω_A_(· | s_t, g_t), then _d[g]_t+1 ∼ _ρ_A_(· | g_{t+1}, ∅).
comments.explanation: While the overall mechanism is described in §3, in this passage the SUB action is said to push _(gt+1, d[g]t+1)_, but it's unclear whether _gt+1_ and _d[g]t+1_ are generated at time _t_ (when the SUB action is taken) or at _t+1_. The transition rules in §4.1 and §4.2 imply that the stack update occurs when the action is taken, so the subgoal must be sampled immediately. However, the notation _gt+1_ suggests it's defined in the next state. This creates index confusion. Was the subgoal sampled based on _st_ and _gt_, or on _st+1_ and _gt+1_? The key equation in section summary says: _g_{t+1} ∼ ω_A(· | s_t, g_t)_, so the timing is at _t_. But the use of _t+1_ subscript implies a delay. This is a subtle but important distinction for reproducibility. While the context from the 'FULL PAPER CONTEXT' resolves this (confirming the sampling is at _t_), within the passage alone a careful reader might misinterpret the causal order. This is particularly important because the agent must act knowing the subgoal before transitioning. The notation should use _g'_ or _g_new_ to avoid temporal misreading.

comments.title: Ambiguity in SUB action subgoal definition
comments.quote: it can ask for a description of its current location (CUR), the goal location (GOAL), or a subgoal location that is on the path from its current location to the goal location (SUB).
comments.explanation: At first glance, this sentence suggests that the subgoal requested by the SUB action is a location *on the path* from the current location to the goal. This implies that the subgoal is spatially intermediate and perhaps determined by the environment’s geometry or shortest path. However, the formal setup does not support this interpretation. According to the framework, the subgoal is sampled from the assistant’s subgoal function ωₐ(sₜ, gₜ), which proposes *any* helpful subgoal — not necessarily one on a geometric path. Moreover, the example in Figure 1 shows requesting 'Find the hallway statue' as a subtask when trying to locate a mug in the kitchen, which is abstract and not necessarily on a navigational path. Thus, the phrase 'on the path' is misleading — it seems to ground the subgoal in physical proximity or trajectory, but the actual framework supports *task decomposition*, not *spatial interpolation*. This could mislead readers into thinking subgoals are route-based waypoints rather than potentially abstract intermediate objectives. While the appendix may clarify (as referenced), the main text should not imply a constraint that isn't formally present.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of GOAL action effect on description stack
comments.quote: The GOAL action replaces the top goal description with d[g]_{t+1}, the new description given by the assistant.
comments.explanation: This sentence appears in §4.2 and describes the effect of the GOAL action. However, in the formal definition of the interaction state and description function, the goal stack G contains full goal entries (g, d[g]), while the agent observes only G[d], the stack of descriptions. The GOAL action is said to 'replace the top goal description'. But does this update *only* the description d[g] while preserving the underlying goal state g? Or does it also change g? The cost function and task completion logic depend on reaching the actual goal state g₁, so if GOAL only updates the description without changing g, this is fine. But if g is also changed, that would constitute a goal redefinition, which is not discussed. The text implies only the description is updated, which aligns with the idea that the agent is refining its understanding, not changing the objective. However, the notation 'replaces the top goal description' could be misread as modifying only d[g]_t, while context suggests the full tuple (g, d[g]) might remain with g unchanged. This is mostly resolved by the assumption that the assistant is helpful and accurate, and that only SUB pushes new (g, d[g]) pairs. Still, the wording is ambiguous: 'replace the top goal description' could mean overwriting d[g] within the existing goal entry, which is plausible, but this mechanism isn't formally specified. This leaves open how updates to descriptions without state changes are represented in G — are (g, d[g]) pairs mutable? The deterministic description function ρ̄ suggests descriptions are derived from states, but here d[g] is actively updated independently. This tension isn't resolved and could confuse readers about whether description and state are decoupled.

comments.title: Sparse description filtering based on 'most frequent objects' lacks definition
comments.quote: we remove the features of objects that are not in the top 100 most frequent objects, emulating an imperfect object detector module.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that sparse descriptions filter out objects not in the 'top 100 most frequent objects', but it does not define what this frequency refers to: frequency in the training data? in the environment? across tasks? This is a crucial design choice because it determines how the sparsity is structured — if it's object frequency in the agent’s pre-training data, then the sparse setting biases toward familiar objects; if it's global in the simulator, it may not align with agent experience. Moreover, the threshold of 100 is arbitrary and unexplained. While the intent — emulating a flawed detector — is clear and valid, the method is underspecified. Without knowing how these 'frequent objects' were determined, it's hard to assess the realism or reproducibility of the setting. This is exacerbated by the claim that this setup makes the problem 'not necessarily easier' than image input, which depends heavily on what objects are retained. The omission affects both interpretability and reproducibility, though it doesn’t invalidate the core findings.

comments.title: Underspecified human subgoal generation mechanism
comments.quote: Detail about how the subgoals are determined is in the Appendix.
comments.explanation: The main text refers readers to the Appendix for subgoal determination, but in the provided context, this detail is absent. Given that SUB is a core action enabling hierarchical planning and significant performance gains (Table 1), its mechanism is critical. For instance, are subgoals on the shortest path? Are they rooms en route? Are they salient landmarks? The effectiveness of requesting subgoals — especially the 7× improvement claim — hinges on the quality and relevance of these subgoals. Without this, the reader cannot judge whether the gains are due to the policy learning or the oracle-like quality of subgoals. While referencing an Appendix is common, in a conference submission under review, key mechanisms should at least be sketched in the main paper, especially when they directly influence evaluation outcomes. This omission makes it difficult to assess the generalizability or fairness of the comparison.

comments.title: Misleading phrasing about request-fulfilling success
comments.quote: We also determine success in _request-fulfilling_ , where the agent successfully fulfills a request if it navigates to any node that is within two meters of an object that matches the request.
comments.explanation: The text states that request-fulfilling success is achieved when the agent reaches 'any node within two meters of an object that matches the request'. However, in the problem setup, the agent is trying to reach a *specific imagined goal location* (a node), not just any matching object. The phrasing suggests that proximity to *any* matching object suffices, but if that object is not at the human's intended location, the agent shouldn't count as request-fulfilled. However, the definition of 'matching the request' — e.g., 'a mug in a kitchen' — likely means objects of the correct type in the correct room. Given the evaluation environment with discrete nodes, it's plausible that being near *a* correct object is equivalent to fulfilling the high-level intent, even if not at the imagined spot. Moreover, the paper distinguishes 'goal-finding' (exact location) from 'request-fulfilling' (approximate), so the latter is intended to measure functional success rather than precision. Thus, the statement is likely correct as an *alternative* evaluation metric. The wording could be misread as conflating *any* matching object with *the* target, but context resolves this: the two metrics are complementary. The paper gets this right — the phrasing is just a bit loose. No real error remains.

comments.title: Inconsistent success rate improvement claims between text and table
comments.quote: Aided by our RL-learned interaction policy, the agent observes a substantial \sim 2 \times increase in success rate on UNSEENSTR, \sim 5 \times on UNSEENOBJ, and \sim 7 \times on UNSEENENV, compared to when performing tasks using only the operation policy.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the claim of a \sim7\times improvement on UNSEENENV appears plausible given the context. However, checking Table 2 reveals that the RL-learned policy with stack size 2 achieves 21.6% success on UNSEENENV. A \sim7\times improvement implies the base operation policy (with no interaction) achieved approximately 21.6 / 7 \approx 3.1\%. But the stack size 1 row (no subgoals) reports 12.5% success, not 3.1\%. Even if we interpret 'performing tasks using only the operation policy' as using no requests at all (stack size 1 may still include CUR/GOAL), the text states that stack size 1 has 1.9 GOAL and 5.1 CUR actions — meaning it *does* request information, contradicting 'only the operation policy'. The rule-based baseline is mentioned later (+7.1% on UNSEENENV), but that's an absolute gain, not multiplicative. Thus, the \times7 claim is inconsistent with the tabulated data: 21.6 / 12.5 \approx 1.7\times, not 7\times. This undermines the central claim of a 7\times boost mentioned in the abstract and here. The authors may have intended to compare against a zero-request variant not shown in the table, but that version is neither defined nor presented.

comments.title: Contradiction between stated policy behavior and table data on GOAL requests
comments.quote: The average number of GOAL actions does not vary, showing that the interaction policy has correctly learned that making more than one goal-clarifying request is unnecessary.
comments.explanation: The prose claims the average number of GOAL actions is stable across tasks, supporting the interpretation that one goal clarification suffices. However, Table 2 shows the number of GOAL actions varies with stack size: 1.9 (stack 1), 1.0 (stack 2), 1.0 (stack 3). While stack 2 and 3 are consistent, stack 1 uses nearly twice as many. If these averages are computed across the same task types (UNSEENSTR, etc.), then the number of GOAL actions *does* vary — by design of the policy across configurations. The authors may mean the number is stable *for a fixed policy* across task types, but the text says 'as the difficulty of the evaluation condition increases', implying comparison across UNSEENSTR to UNSEENENV. No data is provided to support that claim. This overgeneralizes from likely validation-set behavior without quantification.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about dense-description policy being outperformed
comments.quote: In unseen environments, with its capability of requesting subgoals, the agent impressively doubles the success rate of the operation policy that has access to dense descriptions.
comments.explanation: This statement claims the interaction policy (sparse inputs, but can request help) outperforms the operation policy that always has dense current-state and goal descriptions. However, the paper's earlier summary states: 'Dense descriptions significantly boost operation policy performance: ~2× gain on UNSEENSTR, ~5× on UNSEENOBJ, ~3× on UNSEENENV.' This implies the dense-description policy is *stronger* than the sparse one. The current passage then claims the interacting (sparse-based) policy *doubles* the performance of that stronger dense-policy — an even greater leap. But no data supports this. Table 2's highest UNSEENENV score is 33.5% (stack 3), so the 'dense operation policy' would need to be below 16.75% to be 'doubled'. Yet stack size 1 (which includes many CUR/GOAL requests, possibly simulating dense access) already achieves 12.5%, and likely increases with dense inputs. Without explicit results for the 'dense d[s] and d[g]' policy, this claim is unjustified and contradicts expectations from earlier claims about dense inputs helping.

comments.title: Mislabeling in Figure 3 caption due to OCR error
comments.quote: Figure 3: Analyzing the effect of simultaneously varying the cost of the CUR,UR,, GOAL,OAL,, SUB, DO actions
comments.explanation: The caption contains clear OCR artifacts: 'CUR,UR,,' and 'GOAL,OAL,,' are obviously malformed. The intended text is almost certainly 'CUR, GOAL, SUB, DO'. The surrounding figures and context confirm these are the four action types whose costs are being varied. While the meaning is recoverable, the repetition ('UR' after 'CUR') suggests a scanning error. Since the instruction says to flag only if it would be an issue even under the most plausible correction, and the correction is unambiguous, this is not a real problem. The caption's intent is clear, and no reader would be misled.

comments.title: Ambiguity in comparison to 'dense-d[s]-and-d[g] baseline'
comments.quote: Compared to a policy that calls GOAL at the beginning and calls CUR at every step (which is equivalent to the dense- _d[s]_ -and- _d[g]_ baseline), our policy achieves higher success rate on UNSEENENV while making two times fewer requests.
comments.explanation: The authors equate frequent CUR + one GOAL with 'dense d[s] and d[g]' access. However, the dense description is defined as including room name and top-M object features, while sparse restricts to top-100 frequent objects. The policy described (CUR every step) provides up-to-date sparse current-state descriptions repeatedly, not dense ones. Unless the human provides *dense* descriptions in response to CUR requests (which is allowed by the framework but not confirmed in experiments), this equivalence does not hold. The PAPER SUMMARY says humans return dense descriptions upon request — so this *may* be valid. Given that assumption, the equivalence is justified: repeated CUR requests with dense returns simulate always-having dense d[s], and initial GOAL gives dense d[g]. Context resolves this, but the statement is misleading without emphasizing that human-provided descriptions are dense.

comments.title: Inconsistent success rate trend in Table 2 vs prose claim about UNSEENOBJ
comments.quote: On UNSEENOBJ, the RL-learned policy has not closed the gap with the operation policy that performs tasks with dense descriptions.
comments.explanation: Table 2 shows UNSEENOBJ success rates of 78.4% (stack 1), 77.6% (stack 2), 78.6% (stack 3). These are all very high and nearly the same. The prose suggests the dense-description policy is stronger, but there's no data on what that policy achieves. If stack 1 — which uses many CUR and GOAL actions — already reaches 78.4%, and the dense policy (which should be at least as good) is the benchmark, then the gap appears *closed*. The claim that the gap 'has not closed' implies the dense policy performs significantly better, but no evidence supports this. At face value, the learned interaction policy with stack 3 *exceeds* stack 1 on UNSEENOBJ. This suggests either the comparison is misstated or the dense-policy performance is not being properly contextualized.

comments.title: Overstatement of subgoal request timing based on incomplete data
comments.quote: The agent makes most SUB requests in the middle of an episode, after its has attempted but failed to accomplish the main goals.
comments.explanation: The authors justify this by pointing to Figure 2b, which shows a peak in SUB actions around the middle of the episode. However, the figure alone does not establish causality or intent — it doesn't prove SUB requests are made *after failed attempts*, only that they cluster mid-episode. The agent could be proactively requesting subgoals mid-task for efficiency, not as a failure recovery. The claim attributes internal reasoning ('after failed') without evidence from action sequences or belief states. While plausible, this interpretive leap goes beyond what the plot shows. A more conservative statement would describe the temporal distribution without inferring cause.

comments.title: Missing clarification on what 'operation policy' refers to in comparisons
comments.quote: compared to when performing tasks using only the operation policy
comments.explanation: The term 'operation policy' is used precisely in the framework: it's \hat{\pi}, the low-level navigation policy. But 'performing tasks using only the operation policy' suggests no interaction policy at all — just blind execution. However, the interaction policy \psi_\theta could still call DO actions and use \hat{\pi} without communication. So technically, even the interacting agent relies on the operation policy. The intended meaning is likely 'without requesting information' (i.e., no CUR/GOAL/SUB). But without stating this, a reader might confuse whether \hat{\pi} alone can complete tasks. Context from Section 3 clarifies this, but the phrasing risks ambiguity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Definition of Interaction Policy Input Space
comments.quote: Our formulation does not specify the input space of the interaction policy, as this space depends on how the agent implements its goal memory (i.e. how it stores and retrieves the subgoals).
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement appears reasonable—after all, the interaction policy's input should depend on implementation choices like the goal stack. However, in §4.1, the interaction policy is formally defined as ψθ : B̄ → ∆(A), where B̄ is the set of interaction belief states. This implies that the input is a belief over S̄, which includes the full goal stack G (with true goal states), yet the agent only observes G[d], the stack of descriptions. There is a mismatch: the belief state must be computable from observable quantities, but G is unobservable. While the description function ρ̄ outputs (d[s], G[d]), which is observable, the belief state b̄t must summarize the partial execution based only on these observations. The concern is whether the belief state representation can actually capture the necessary information about G given that only G[d] is observed. The paper assumes a recurrent model (per §2), so this may be resolved in practice via RNN-based belief tracking. However, the theoretical formulation suggests access to unobserved state components. This creates a subtle but important ambiguity in the formalism: the belief state should summarize ρ̄(s̄1:t), not s̄t directly. Since the agent never sees G (only G[d]), defining the belief over S̄ = S × D × GL is misleading unless properly qualified. The issue is not fatal—context suggests they mean the belief over observations—but the formal setup risks leading a reader to believe the agent has access to ground-truth goal states in the stack.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Goal Stack Size Notation in Related Work Summary
comments.quote: HARI can be viewed as a strict extension of these frameworks. It allows the human to specify not only subgoals, but also any additional information about the current state and the goal that agent can interpret.
comments.explanation: The passage discusses HARI’s advantages over previous work, emphasizing its support for multiple types of requests (CUR, GOAL, SUB). However, it omits mention of the hierarchical stack (L=3) that enables recursive subgoal requests—a key contribution highlighted in the paper summary. While not an error per se, the omission in this comparative discussion could mislead readers about the novelty of HARI's hierarchical structure. The paper’s experimental results stress that increasing stack depth improves performance in UNSEENENV (+11.9%), indicating that deeper stacks are a significant advancement over prior frameworks that only allow one-level subgoals. Yet, the Related Work section frames HARI’s advantage solely in terms of richer request types rather than hierarchical composition. This downplays a core innovation. Given that the authors explicitly analyze stack size effects elsewhere, it is reasonable to expect that the comparison to prior work would highlight this structural difference. The context resolves the technical accuracy (the claim about multiple intentions is correct), but the framing underrepresents HARI's full contribution.

comments.title: Underdefined Subgoal Fulfillment Criterion in Request-Fulfilling Success
comments.quote: We also determine success in _request-fulfilling_ , where the agent successfully fulfills a request if it navigates to any node that is within two meters of an object that matches the request.
comments.explanation: This definition appears late in the paper (Section 5), but no subsequent analysis references 'request-fulfilling' success, and the key claims focus on 'goal-finding' success (exact location match). The issue arises from ambiguity: if multiple nodes satisfy the 'within two meters' condition, does the agent need to reach one of them *after* receiving the SUB request, or is any such visit sufficient? Crucially, this metric is introduced but not used in reported results. The PAPER SUMMARY only discusses goal-finding rates and never mentions request-fulfilling performance. This raises concern about whether this is a meaningful evaluation criterion or just a passing definition. However, since no claims are made about it, and the main evaluation uses goal-finding (which is clearly defined), the omission of analysis might simply reflect focus. Still, introducing a success metric without using it risks confusing the reader about what constitutes task success. A careful reader might misinterpret that both metrics are reported, when only one is used. The underuse of this defined metric makes the method underspecified in terms of evaluation reporting.

comments.title: Potential Misalignment Between CUR Action Definition and Observation Model
comments.quote: CUR: requests a new description of st and receives d[s] t+1 ∼ ρA(· | st, d[s]t);
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems consistent: the agent requests a new current-state description, and the assistant provides it using ρA conditioned on st and the previous d[s]t. But later, in §4.1, the description function ρ̄ is defined as deterministic: ρ̄(s, d[s], G) = (d[s], G[d]). This suggests that d[s]t is part of the observed state and assumed fixed unless updated by an action. The concern is whether d[s]t+1 after a CUR action becomes the new observation for the next step. If so, then Ts (defined in §4.3) must account for this change. The text says: 'The description d[s]t may vary when the agent moves to a new operation state (by taking the DO action) or requests a new description of st (by taking the CUR action)', which resolves the issue—CUR does update the observed description. However, the stochasticity of d[s]t+1 ∼ ρA(· | st, d[s]t) appears to conflict with the later deterministic ρ̄. But ρ̄ maps the full state s̄ to observation d̄, while ρA governs the assistant’s response—a conditional distribution over new descriptions. So ρ̄ applies after the transition, once d[s]t+1 is sampled. Thus, the stochastic update via ρA is part of the transition dynamics, not the observation function. The resolution lies in distinguishing between the environment’s stochastic response (ρA) and the observation mapping (ρ̄). The model remains coherent: the assistant samples d[s]t+1 stochastically, then ρ̄ outputs it deterministically. No actual contradiction exists—context resolves it—but the potential confusion arises from overlapping use of 'description' in different functional roles.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and conceptually innovative hierarchical reinforcement learning framework—HARI—for enabling agents to learn both *when* and *what* to ask from humans during task execution. The work makes a strong contribution by extending the standard POMDP formulation to support structured, goal-driven communication, addressing a critical gap in human-agent collaboration: the need for faithful, self-aware, and contextually appropriate information-seeking behavior. The writing is generally clear and logically organized, with a thoughtful progression from motivation through technical formulation to empirical evaluation. The use of Gricean maxims as a guiding principle for designing cooperative communication lends philosophical rigor and underscores the paper’s emphasis on *intrinsic*, needs-based queries rather than superficial imitation of human language patterns. The experimental results are compelling, particularly the 7× improvement in task success rate and the demonstration of generalization to unseen environments via subgoal requests. However, a major limitation lies in the lack of clarity around the model’s architecture and learning algorithm—specifically, how the hierarchical policy is trained end-to-end (or whether it relies on manual decomposition), and how uncertainty or "self-perception" is formally operationalized within the agent. While the framework claims to enable faithful communication grounded in the agent’s own limitations, the mechanisms by which the agent learns to recognize these limitations (e.g., via internal belief states, prediction errors, or auxiliary losses) are under-specified. Additionally, the simulation environment appears highly abstracted, and the nature of human responses (i.e., how descriptions are generated and parsed) is not sufficiently detailed, raising concerns about the realism and reproducibility of the setup. These omissions weaken the technical grounding of an otherwise promising and impactful approach. Strengthening the methodological exposition and providing clearer distinctions between the policy components (e.g., meta-controller for information requests vs. worker policy for task execution) would significantly improve the paper’s scientific rigor and accessibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about success rate improvement in abstract and introduction
comments.quote: Results on a simulated human-assisted navigation problem demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework: aided with an interaction policy learned by our method, a navigation policy achieves up to a 7 _×_ improvement in task success rate compared to performing tasks only by itself.
comments.explanation: At first, the claim of a 7× improvement in task success rate seems clear and impactful. However, later in the introduction, the same result is restated as 'the ability to ask for help improves the agent’s success rate by 7 _×_ higher compared to performing tasks only by itself.' The use of 'by 7× higher' is problematic: 'improves by 7×' typically means an 8-fold absolute increase (i.e., original + 7× original), while 'improves to 7×' or 'is 7× higher' would mean a 7-fold increase. The phrase 'by 7× higher' is linguistically incoherent—it combines additive and multiplicative phrasings. Given that both the abstract and the introduction use similar but slightly different phrasings ('7× improvement' vs '7× higher'), it raises concern about whether the authors mean the success rate is multiplied by 7, or increased by 7 times its value (i.e., 8× the baseline). While '7× improvement' is often interpreted in practice as '7 times the baseline' (not '+7×'), the later phrasing 'by 7× higher' suggests potential confusion in quantification. This is not resolved by context because both statements are central empirical claims. A careful reader might misinterpret the magnitude of the gain, potentially inflating the perceived performance. Standard usage in ML literature (e.g., 'n× improvement') usually means multiplicative scaling, so the first statement is acceptable, but the second wording undermines precision.

comments.title: Ambiguity in subgoal request mechanism and its modeling
comments.quote: The agent in HARI is equipped with three information-seeking intentions: in every step, it can choose to request more information about (i) its current state, (ii) the goal state, or (iii) a subgoal state which, if reached, helps it make progress on the current task.
comments.explanation: The prose suggests that the agent can request information about a subgoal state—but a subgoal has not yet been defined at this point in the text. This initially creates confusion: is the agent requesting information about an existing subgoal, or asking the assistant to *propose* a subgoal? The latter is clearly the intended meaning, as later sections (e.g., §3 and §4.2) clarify that the SUB action triggers the assistant to propose a new subgoal via ωA. However, the phrasing 'request more information about... a subgoal state' incorrectly implies the subgoal already exists and the agent wants more details on it, rather than initiating the creation of a new subgoal. This misleads the reader into thinking the subgoal is pre-specified or under the agent’s control, when in fact it is sampled by the assistant. Context eventually resolves this, but in a key conceptual description like this—especially in the introduction—it’s important to be precise. A better phrasing would be 'request the assistant to propose a helpful subgoal (a state which, if reached, helps...)' to reflect agency lies with the assistant.

comments.title: Incoherent text fragment after 'These de-' due to OCR or line break issue
comments.quote: These de-
comments.explanation: The passage ends the paragraph abruptly with 'These de-', which is clearly a line-break artifact from 'descriptions'. Given that the following line begins with 'scriptions will be incorporated...', this is almost certainly a formatting issue from the PDF-to-text conversion, not an author error. The OCR likely split 'descriptions' across two lines. No substantive issue remains after reading the full sentence: 'These descriptions will be incorporated...'. As per instructions, we do not flag formatting or incomplete text at boundaries. However, the issue is noted in case downstream processing treats this as a missing word. Since it is clearly recoverable from context and the full word appears immediately after, and given that the full paper context confirms the continuity, this does not reflect on the authors’ writing.

comments.title: Misleading figure caption description of stack behavior
comments.quote: When the agent receives a (sub)task, it pushes the (sub)task to the top of the stack. When it wants to stop executing a (sub)task, it pops the (sub)task from the stack (e.g., at location D).
comments.explanation: The caption says the agent pops a (sub)task 'when it wants to stop executing' it, implying that DONE pops any arbitrary subtask on demand. However, later formal definition in §4.2 states that DONE only pops the top task on the stack—consistent with stack semantics. The phrase 'wants to stop executing a (sub)task' is ambiguous: it could imply the agent can select which subtask to terminate, breaking stack discipline. But the example at location D shows popping the current (top) subtask, and the full mechanism uses a stack with LIFO order. Thus, the intended behavior is consistent, but the wording in the caption could mislead a reader into thinking the agent has random access to subtasks on the stack. Given that the formal model is correct and the figure example is accurate, the issue is one of phrasing. Still, for a reader relying on the caption before seeing the formalism, the potential for misunderstanding is real, especially given real-world agents sometimes allow pausing/resuming arbitrary subroutines.

comments.title: Use of 'gt = g1' in description of DONE action suggests logical error
comments.quote: If _gt_ is a main goal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the task episode ends. If _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1), the agent may choose a new goal to follow.
comments.explanation: This passage from section 3 creates a serious concern: it says 'if _gt_ is a subgoal ( _gt_ = _g_ 1)', but _g_ 1 is defined as the main goal. This would imply a subgoal equals the main goal, which is contradictory. Initially, this appears to be a typo—likely the second equality should be _gt_ ≠ _g_ 1. Checking the context, the first clause correctly identifies main goal with _gt_ = _g_ 1. The second clause cannot mean _gt_ = _g_ 1 if it is a subgoal, unless the subgoal is the main goal, which defeats the purpose. However, the parentheses suggest an attempt to define the condition, not describe a subcase. Given the stack mechanism in §4.2, subgoals are pushed onto the stack and become _gt_, while _g_ 1 remains the original goal. Therefore, subgoals are by definition different from _g_ 1 (or at least not required to be equal). This is likely a transcription or OCR error where '≠' was misread as '='. Even under most plausible correction (≠), the syntax 'gt is a subgoal (gt ≠ g1)' would still be awkward. But without correction, it’s logically inconsistent. Since the rest of the framework assumes _g_ 1 ≠ subgoals and stack-based execution depends on distinguishing them, this is a real notational error. However, due to clear context and figure, the intent is recoverable. Still, it’s a moderate risk for confusion.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of goal description notation in task definition
comments.quote: Each _task_ in the environment is defined as a tuple ( _s_ 1 _, g_ 1 _, d[g]_ 1[)][where] _[s]_[1][is][the][start][state,] _[g]_[1][is][the] goal state, and _d[g]_ 1[is a limited description of] _[ g]_[1][.][Initially, a task][ (] _[s]_[1] _[, g]_[1] _[, d][g]_ 1[)][ is sampled from a task]
comments.explanation: At first glance, the notation _d[g]_1 appears to be consistently used for the goal description. However, later in the paper (§3 and §4), the notation shifts to _d[g]_t to denote the evolving goal description over time, which is appropriate in the HARI framework. The issue arises in this passage because the opening clause reads '( _s_1, g_1, d[g]_1)' as if _d[g]_1 is part of the initial task sampling — which is correct — but the sentence is cut off mid-way ('sampled from a task') due to OCR or formatting, making it appear as if the task distribution is also named 'task', creating a circular reference. Given that the full paper context confirms that tasks are sampled from a task distribution, and that _d[g]_1 is indeed fixed initially, the intent is clear. However, the truncation (likely due to line break and hyphenation) 'task]' → 'task' and the missing closing parenthesis introduce syntactic ambiguity. This would mislead a careful reader about whether _d[g]_1 is sampled or deterministic. While in context it's resolvable, the construction is fragile.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the evolution of goal state under SUB requests
comments.quote: (c) SUB: requests a description of a subgoal _gt_ +1 and receives _d[g] t_ +1 _[∼][ρ][A]_[ (] _[· |][ g][t]_[+1] _[,][ ∅]_[)][ where] _gt_ +1 _∼ ωA_ ( _· | st, gt_ ) and _∅_ is an empty description.
comments.explanation: The SUB request leads to a new subgoal _g_{t+1}_ sampled from _ωA(· | s_t, g_t)_, and its description is drawn from _ρA(· | g_{t+1}, ∅)_. This is consistent with the framework’s design where subgoals are proposed by the assistant and descriptions are generated based on an empty prior. However, the text does not clarify whether _g_t_ (the current goal) is replaced by _g_{t+1}_ or if _g_{t+1}_ is merely pushed onto a stack. Crucially, this passage (§3) introduces the SUB request before the goal stack is formally defined in §4.2, so a reader might misinterpret this as overwriting the goal rather than hierarchical task management. While Figure 1 and §4.2 later clarify the stack-based mechanism, the lack of forward reference or caveat here creates ambiguity about the agent’s goal semantics during a SUB request. This could mislead a reader about whether the original goal is abandoned or suspended.

comments.title: Misleading implication about fixed description function in standard POMDP
comments.quote: Moreover, the description function _ρ_ and the goal description _d[g]_ 1[are assumed to be fixed during a task execution.
comments.explanation: The assertion that _ρ_ is fixed is standard in traditional POMDPs, but in this paper, _ρ_ has been redefined as a general 'description function' that includes communication-mediated inputs (per footnote [1]). The authors correctly distinguish their use of 'description' from traditional 'observation', but here they conflate the interpretation: in a standard POMDP, _ρ_ is fixed *by definition*, but in HARI, _ρ_ is supplemented by _ρA_, creating a time-varying effective description process. The sentence risks leading a reader to believe that *in HARI* the original _ρ_ remains the only source of descriptions and is immutable, when in fact _ρ_ coexists with _ρA_ as complementary sources. However, since the paper clearly separates _ρ_ (sensor-based) and _ρA_ (assistant-based), and later defines agent requests as accessing _ρA_, the confusion is resolved by context. Still, the phrasing could imply that _ρ_ itself is modified, which it is not. A more precise statement would clarify that _ρ_ is fixed, but the agent can access additional descriptions via _ρA_ through requests.

comments.title: Missing subscript alignment in description expectation
comments.quote: Given _d[s] t_ and _d[g] t_[,][the][agent][then][makes][a][decision] _[a][t][∈A]_[,][transitions][to][the][next][state] _[s][t]_[+1] _[∼][T]_[(] _[s][t][, a][t]_[)][,] and receives a cost _ct_ ≜ _c_ ( _st, at_ )
comments.explanation: The agent receives a description _d[s]_t ∼ ρ(s_t), makes a decision, and transitions to s_{t+1}.
